epigraph.text:
Make me to know your ways, O Lord; teach me your 
paths.

content.text:
What about the integral
\[
 \int \frac{\sqrt{4 - x^{2}}}{x^{2}}\dx\,?
\]
The presence of $\sqrt{4 - x^{2}}$ blocks any direct $u$-substitution:
setting $u = 4 - x^{2}$ gives $du = -2x\dx$, but no free factor of $x$ in
the numerator pairs with it. The half-angle and power-reduction techniques
of Section [sec:trig-integrals] do not apply either. We need a
fundamentally different idea.
Each substitution is designed to exploit one of the Pythagorean identities.

content.title: Trigonometric Substitutions
content.content:
{1.6}

| Expression | Substitution | Identity Used | Range of $\theta$ |
| $\sqrt{a^{2} - x^{2}}$ | $x = a\sin\theta$ | $1 - \sin^{2}\theta = \cos^{2}\theta$ | $-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\le\theta\le\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ |
| $\sqrt{a^{2} + x^{2}}$ | $x = a\tan\theta$ | $1 + \tan^{2}\theta = \sec^{2}\theta$ | $-\dfrac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ |
| $\sqrt{x^{2} - a^{2}}$ | $x = a\sec\theta$ | $\sec^{2}\theta - 1 = \tan^{2}\theta$ | $0\le\theta<\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ or $\pi\le\theta<\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ |

{1.0}
The restrictions on $\theta$ ensure the substitution is one-to-one and the
square root is non-negative.

content.title: Always convert back to $x$
content.content:
After integrating in terms of $\theta$, you must express the answer in terms
of the original variable $x$. The **reference triangle** is the
standard tool for this conversion.

content.title: Watch for radicals that aren't there
content.content:
Trigonometric substitution works even when there is no explicit square root,
as long as the expression $a^{2} - x^{2}$, $a^{2} + x^{2}$, or $x^{2} - a^{2}$
appears. For example, $\int \frac{dx}{(x^{2}+4)^{2}}$ benefits from
$x = 2\tan\theta$.

content.text:
After integrating in terms of $\theta$, we need to convert back to $x$.
The **reference triangle** makes this conversion systematic.

content.title: Constructing the Reference Triangle
content.content:
1. From your substitution (e.g., $x = a\sin\theta$), identify
 the trig ratio: $\sin\theta = x/a$.

2. Draw a right triangle where this ratio holds. Label the sides.

3. Use the Pythagorean theorem to find the third side.

4. Read off any trig function of $\theta$ directly from the triangle.

content.text:
For instance, if $x = 2\sin\theta$, then $\sin\theta = x/2$, so the
opposite side is $x$ and the hypotenuse is $2$. The adjacent side is
$\sqrt{4 - x^{2}}$. From this triangle:
\[
 \cos\theta = \frac{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{2}, \quad
 \tan\theta = \frac{x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}, \quad
 \cot\theta = \frac{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{x}.
\]

content.caption: Reference triangle for √(a² − x²)
content.alt: Right triangle reference diagram for the substitution x = a sin θ, used when the integrand contains √(a² − x²). The triangle has hypotenuse a, opposite side x, and adjacent side √(a² − x²). The angle θ is at the origin. Formulas shown: sin θ = x/a, cos θ = √(a² − x²)/a, and dx = a cos θ dθ.

content.text: When you see $\sqrt{a^{2} - x^{2}}$, substitute $x = a\sin\theta$.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{\sqrt{4 - x^{2}}}{x^{2}}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 2\sin\theta$, so $dx = 2\cos\theta\,d\theta$ and
$\sqrt{4 - x^{2}} = 2\cos\theta$. Then
align*
 $\sqrt{4 - x^{2}$}{x^{2}}
 &= $\frac{2\cos\theta}{4\sin^{2}$} 2\,d
 = 4^{2}{4^{2}}\,d \\[4pt]
 &= \int \cot^{2}\theta\,d\theta
 = \int (\csc^{2}\theta - 1)\,d\theta \\[4pt]
 &= -\cot\theta - \theta + C.
\end{align*}
\textbf{Converting back.} Since $x = 2\sin\theta$, we have
$\sin\theta = x/2$. Draw a right triangle with opposite side $x$,
hypotenuse $2$, and adjacent side $\sqrt{4-x^{2}}$. Then
$\cot\theta = \sqrt{4-x^{2}}/x$ and $\theta = \arcsin(x/2)$. Therefore,
\[
 \int \frac{\sqrt{4 - x^{2}}}{x^{2}}\dx
 = -\frac{\sqrt{4 - x^{2}}}{x} - \arcsin\!\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sqrt{9 - x^{2}}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 3\sin\theta$, $dx = 3\cos\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{9 - x^{2}} = 3\cos\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sqrt{9 - x^{2}}\dx
 &= \int 3\cos\theta \cdot 3\cos\theta\,d\theta
 = 9\int \cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= 9 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta\right) + C \\
 &= \frac{9\theta}{2} + \frac{9\sin\theta\cos\theta}{2} + C.
\end{aligned}$$
Converting back: $\sin\theta = x/3$, $\cos\theta = \sqrt{9-x^{2}}/3$,
$\theta = \arcsin(x/3)$.
\[
 \int \sqrt{9 - x^{2}}\dx = \frac{9}{2}\arcsin\!\left(\frac{x}{3}\right)
 + \frac{x\sqrt{9-x^{2}}}{2} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{16 - x^{2}}}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 4\sin\theta$, $dx = 4\cos\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{16 - x^{2}} = 4\cos\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{16 - x^{2}}}\dx
 &= \int \frac{16\sin^{2}\theta}{4\cos\theta} \cdot 4\cos\theta\,d\theta
 = 16\int \sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= 16 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta) + C \\
 &= 8\theta - 8\sin\theta\cos\theta + C.
\end{aligned}$$
Converting back: $\theta = \arcsin(x/4)$,
$\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{x}{4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{16-x^{2}}}{4}
= \frac{x\sqrt{16-x^{2}}}{16}$.
\[
 \int \frac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{16 - x^{2}}}\dx
 = 8\arcsin\!\left(\frac{x}{4}\right) - \frac{x\sqrt{16-x^{2}}}{2} + C.
\]

content.title: Area of a circle
content.problem:
Use integration to verify that the area of a circle of radius $r$ is
$\pi r^{2}$.
content.solution:
The upper half of the circle $x^{2} + y^{2} = r^{2}$ is $y = \sqrt{r^{2} -
x^{2}}$. The total area is
\[
 A = 2\defint{-r}{r}\sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}\dx.
\]
By symmetry, $A = 4\defint{0}{r}\sqrt{r^{2}-x^{2}}\dx$. Let
$x = r\sin\theta$, $dx = r\cos\theta\,d\theta$. When $x=0$, $\theta=0$;
when $x=r$, $\theta=\pi/2$.
$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 4\defint{0}{\pi/2} r\cos\theta \cdot r\cos\theta\,d\theta
 = 4r^{2}\defint{0}{\pi/2}\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\[4pt]
 &= 4r^{2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}
 = \pi r^{2}.
\end{aligned}$$
Here we used the result $\defint{0}{\pi/2}\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta
= \pi/4$ from Section [sec:trig-integrals].

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{(a^{2} - x^{2})^{3/2}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = a\sin\theta$, $dx = a\cos\theta\,d\theta$. Then
$(a^{2} - x^{2})^{3/2} = (a^{2}\cos^{2}\theta)^{3/2} = a^{3}\cos^{3}\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dx}{(a^{2} - x^{2})^{3/2}}
 &= \int \frac{a\cos\theta}{a^{3}\cos^{3}\theta}\,d\theta
 = \frac{1}{a^{2}}\int \sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= \frac{\tan\theta}{a^{2}} + C.
\end{aligned}$$
From the triangle: $\tan\theta = x/\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}$.
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{(a^{2} - x^{2})^{3/2}}
 = \frac{x}{a^{2}\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{\sqrt{25 - x^{2}}}{x}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 5\sin\theta$, $dx = 5\cos\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{25-x^{2}} = 5\cos\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{\sqrt{25 - x^{2}}}{x}\dx
 &= \int \frac{5\cos\theta}{5\sin\theta} \cdot 5\cos\theta\,d\theta
 = 5\int \frac{\cos^{2}\theta}{\sin\theta}\,d\theta \\
 &= 5\int \frac{1 - \sin^{2}\theta}{\sin\theta}\,d\theta
 = 5\int (\csc\theta - \sin\theta)\,d\theta \\
 &= 5\bigl(-\ln|\csc\theta + \cot\theta| + \cos\theta\bigr) + C.
\end{aligned}$$
From the triangle: $\csc\theta = 5/x$, $\cot\theta = \sqrt{25-x^{2}}/x$,
$\cos\theta = \sqrt{25-x^{2}}/5$.
\[
 = -5\ln\!\left|\frac{5 + \sqrt{25-x^{2}}}{x}\right| + \sqrt{25-x^{2}} + C.
\]

content.text: When you see $\sqrt{a^{2} + x^{2}}$, substitute $x = a\tan\theta$.

content.caption: Reference triangle for √(a² + x²)
content.alt: Right triangle reference diagram for the substitution x = a tan θ, used when the integrand contains √(a² + x²). The triangle has adjacent side a, opposite side x, and hypotenuse √(a² + x²). The angle θ is at the origin. Formulas shown: tan θ = x/a, sec θ = √(a² + x²)/a, and dx = a sec² θ dθ.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+9}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = 3\tan\theta$, $dx = 3\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$, so
$\sqrt{x^{2}+9} = \sqrt{9\tan^{2}\theta+9} = 3\sec\theta$. Then
align*
 $\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}$+9}}
 &= 3^{2}{3}\,d
 = \,d \\[4pt]
 &= \ln\abs{\sec\theta + \tan\theta} + C.
\end{align*}
\textbf{Converting back.} Since $\tan\theta = x/3$, the reference triangle
has opposite $x$, adjacent $3$, hypotenuse $\sqrt{x^{2}+9}$. So
$\sec\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}+9}/3$. Therefore,
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+9}}
 = \ln\!\left\lvert\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+9}}{3} + \frac{x}{3}\right\rvert + C
 = \ln\!\left\lvert\sqrt{x^{2}+9} + x\right\rvert + C_{1},
\]
where $C_{1} = C - \ln 3$ absorbs the constant.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sqrt{x^{2}+4}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 2\tan\theta$, $dx = 2\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}+4} = 2\sec\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sqrt{x^{2}+4}\dx
 &= \int 2\sec\theta \cdot 2\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta
 = 4\int \sec^{3}\theta\,d\theta.
\end{aligned}$$
Using the result from Section [sec:trig-integrals]:
\[
 \int \sec^{3}\theta\,d\theta
 = \frac{1}{2}(\sec\theta\tan\theta + \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta|) + C.
\]
So:
\[
 \int \sqrt{x^{2}+4}\dx = 2\sec\theta\tan\theta
 + 2\ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C.
\]
Converting: $\tan\theta = x/2$, $\sec\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}+4}/2$.
\[
 = \frac{x\sqrt{x^{2}+4}}{2} + 2\ln\!\left|\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+4}+x}{2}\right|
 + C.
\]
This simplifies to:
\[
 \int \sqrt{x^{2}+4}\dx = \frac{x\sqrt{x^{2}+4}}{2}
 + 2\ln|\sqrt{x^{2}+4}+x| + C_{1}.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+4}}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 2\tan\theta$, $dx = 2\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}+4} = 2\sec\theta$. Then
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+4}}\dx
 &= \int \frac{4\tan^{2}\theta}{2\sec\theta}\cdot 2\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta
 = 4\int \tan^{2}\theta\,\sec\theta\,d\theta.
\end{aligned}$$
Write $\tan^{2}\theta = \sec^{2}\theta - 1$:
\[
 = 4\int (\sec^{3}\theta - \sec\theta)\,d\theta.
\]
Using results from Section [sec:trig-integrals]:
align*
 &= 4 + C \\[4pt]
 &= 2\sec\theta\tan\theta
 - 2\ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta| + C.
\end{align*}
Converting back:
\[
 = \frac{x\sqrt{x^{2}+4}}{2}
 - 2\ln\!\left|\sqrt{x^{2}+4}+x\right| + C_{1}.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{(x^{2}+4)^{3/2}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = 2\tan\theta$, $dx = 2\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$.
Then $(x^{2}+4)^{3/2} = (4\sec^{2}\theta)^{3/2} = 8\sec^{3}\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dx}{(x^{2}+4)^{3/2}}
 &= \int \frac{2\sec^{2}\theta}{8\sec^{3}\theta}\,d\theta
 = \frac{1}{4}\int \cos\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= \frac{\sin\theta}{4} + C.
\end{aligned}$$
From the triangle: $\sin\theta = x/\sqrt{x^{2}+4}$.
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{(x^{2}+4)^{3/2}} = \frac{x}{4\sqrt{x^{2}+4}} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{x^{3}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = \tan\theta$, $dx = \sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}+1} = \sec\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{x^{3}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}\dx
 &= \int \frac{\tan^{3}\theta}{\sec\theta}\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta
 = \int \tan^{3}\theta\sec\theta\,d\theta.
\end{aligned}$$
From Section [sec:trig-integrals], this equals
$\frac{\sec^{3}\theta}{3} - \sec\theta + C$.

Converting: $\sec\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}+1}$.
\[
 \int \frac{x^{3}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}\dx
 = \frac{(x^{2}+1)^{3/2}}{3} - \sqrt{x^{2}+1} + C
 = \frac{(x^{2}+1)^{3/2} - 3\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{3} + C.
\]
This can be factored as $\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}(x^{2}-2)}{3} + C$.

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}{1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = \tan\theta$, $dx = \sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}+1} = \sec\theta$.

When $x = 0$, $\theta = 0$. When $x = 1$, $\theta = \pi/4$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\defint{0}{1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}
 &= \defint{0}^{\pi/4}\frac{\sec^{2}\theta}{\sec\theta}\,d\theta
 = \defint{0}^{\pi/4}\sec\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= \bigl[\ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta|\bigr]_{0}^{\pi/4} \\
 &= \ln|\sqrt{2} + 1| - \ln|1 + 0| = \ln(1 + \sqrt{2}).
\end{aligned}$$

content.text: When you see $\sqrt{x^{2} - a^{2}}$, substitute $x = a\sec\theta$.

content.caption: Reference triangle for √(x² − a²)
content.alt: Right triangle reference diagram for the substitution x = a sec θ, used when the integrand contains √(x² − a²). The triangle has hypotenuse x, adjacent side a, and opposite side √(x² − a²). The angle θ is at the origin. Formulas shown: sec θ = x/a, tan θ = √(x² − a²)/a, and dx = a sec θ tan θ dθ.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{x^{2}\sqrt{x^{2}-25}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = 5\sec\theta$, $dx = 5\sec\theta\,\tan\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}-25} = 5\tan\theta$. Then
align*
 $\frac{dx}{x^{2}$$\sqrt{x^{2}$-25}}
 &= $\frac{5\sec\theta\,\tan\theta}{25\sec^{2}$ 5}
 \,d \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{1}{25}\int \frac{1}{\sec\theta}\,d\theta
 = \frac{1}{25}\int \cos\theta\,d\theta \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{\sin\theta}{25} + C.
\end{align*}
\textbf{Converting back.} Since $\sec\theta = x/5$, the reference triangle
has hypotenuse $x$, adjacent $5$, opposite $\sqrt{x^{2}-25}$. So
$\sin\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}-25}/x$. Therefore,
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{x^{2}\sqrt{x^{2}-25}}
 = \frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-25}}{25x} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-9}}{x}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = 3\sec\theta$, $dx = 3\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}-9} = 3\tan\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-9}}{x}\dx
 &= \int \frac{3\tan\theta}{3\sec\theta} \cdot 3\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta
 = 3\int \tan^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= 3(\tan\theta - \theta) + C.
\end{aligned}$$
Converting: $\tan\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}-9}/3$,
$\theta = \operatorname{arcsec}(x/3) = \arccos(3/x)$.
\[
 \int \frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-9}}{x}\dx = \sqrt{x^{2}-9} - 3\arccos\!\left(\frac{3}{x}\right) + C.
\]
(Or equivalently, $\sqrt{x^{2}-9} - 3\operatorname{arcsec}(x/3) + C$.)

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{x\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = \sec\theta$, $dx = \sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}-1} = \tan\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dx}{x\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}
 &= \int \frac{\sec\theta\tan\theta}{\sec\theta \cdot \tan\theta}\,d\theta
 = \int d\theta = \theta + C.
\end{aligned}$$
Since $\sec\theta = x$, we have $\theta = \operatorname{arcsec}(x)$.
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{x\sqrt{x^{2}-1}} = \operatorname{arcsec}(x) + C
 = \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}$
content.solution:
Let $x = 2\sec\theta$, $dx = 2\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}-4} = 2\tan\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}-4}}
 &= \int \frac{2\sec\theta\tan\theta}{2\tan\theta}\,d\theta
 = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C.
\end{aligned}$$
Converting: $\sec\theta = x/2$, $\tan\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}-4}/2$.
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}-4}} = \ln\!\left|\frac{x + \sqrt{x^{2}-4}}{2}\right| + C
 = \ln|x + \sqrt{x^{2}-4}| + C_{1}.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x^{3}\sqrt{x^{2}-1}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $x = \sec\theta$, $dx = \sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta$,
$\sqrt{x^{2}-1} = \tan\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int x^{3}\sqrt{x^{2}-1}\dx
 &= \int \sec^{3}\theta \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta
 = \int \sec^{4}\theta\tan^{2}\theta\,d\theta.
\end{aligned}$$
Write $\sec^{4}\theta = \sec^{2}\theta(1 + \tan^{2}\theta)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int \sec^{2}\theta\tan^{2}\theta(1 + \tan^{2}\theta)\,d\theta
 = \int (\tan^{2}\theta + \tan^{4}\theta)\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta.
\end{aligned}$$
Let $u = \tan\theta$, $du = \sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta$:
\[
 = \int (u^{2} + u^{4})\,du = \frac{u^{3}}{3} + \frac{u^{5}}{5} + C.
\]
Converting: $u = \tan\theta = \sqrt{x^{2}-1}$.
\[
 \int x^{3}\sqrt{x^{2}-1}\dx
 = \frac{(x^{2}-1)^{3/2}}{3} + \frac{(x^{2}-1)^{5/2}}{5} + C.
\]

content.text:
Many integrands contain expressions like $ax^{2} + bx + c$ under a radical.
To apply trig substitution, we first **complete the square** to create
one of the three standard forms.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}-4x+8}}$
content.solution:
Complete the square: $x^{2}-4x+8 = (x-2)^{2}+4$. Let $w = x-2$:
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+4}}
 = \int \frac{dw}{\sqrt{w^{2}+4}}.
\]
This is of the form $\sqrt{w^{2}+a^{2}}$ with $a = 2$. Let
$w = 2\tan\theta$:
align*
 $\frac{dw}{\sqrt{w^{2}$+4}}
 &= 2^{2}{2}\,d
 = \,d \\[4pt]
 &= \ln\abs{\sec\theta+\tan\theta} + C.
\end{align*}
Converting back: $\tan\theta = w/2 = (x-2)/2$,
$\sec\theta = \sqrt{w^{2}+4}/2 = \sqrt{x^{2}-4x+8}/2$.
\[
 = \ln\!\left\lvert\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-4x+8} + x - 2}{2}\right\rvert + C
 = \ln\bigl\lvert\sqrt{x^{2}-4x+8}+x-2\bigr\rvert + C_{1}.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{5+4x-x^{2}}}$
content.solution:
Complete the square: $5+4x-x^{2} = -(x^{2}-4x-5) = -(x^{2}-4x+4-9)
= 9 - (x-2)^{2}$. Let $w = x-2$:
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{9-w^{2}}}.
\]
This is of the form $\sqrt{a^{2}-w^{2}}$ with $a = 3$. Let
$w = 3\sin\theta$, $dw = 3\cos\theta\,d\theta$:
\[
 \int \frac{3\cos\theta}{3\cos\theta}\,d\theta
 = \int d\theta = \theta + C
 = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{w}{3}\right) + C
 = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{x-2}{3}\right) + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+6x+13}}$
content.solution:
Complete the square: $x^{2}+6x+13 = (x+3)^{2} + 4$. Let $w = x+3$:
\[
 \int \frac{dw}{\sqrt{w^{2}+4}}.
\]
With $w = 2\tan\theta$:
\[
 = \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C
 = \ln\!\left|\sqrt{w^{2}+4} + w\right| + C_{1}
 = \ln\!\left|\sqrt{x^{2}+6x+13} + x + 3\right| + C_{1}.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sqrt{2x - x^{2}}\dx$
content.solution:
Complete the square: $2x - x^{2} = -(x^{2} - 2x) = -(x^{2} - 2x + 1 - 1)
= 1 - (x-1)^{2}$. Let $w = x - 1$:
\[
 \int \sqrt{1 - w^{2}}\,dw.
\]
Let $w = \sin\theta$, $dw = \cos\theta\,d\theta$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sqrt{1 - \sin^{2}\theta}\cos\theta\,d\theta
 &= \int \cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= \frac{\theta}{2} + \frac{\sin\theta\cos\theta}{2} + C \\
 &= \frac{\arcsin w}{2} + \frac{w\sqrt{1-w^{2}}}{2} + C \\
 &= \frac{\arcsin(x-1)}{2} + \frac{(x-1)\sqrt{2x-x^{2}}}{2} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{(x^{2}+2x+5)^{3/2}}$
content.solution:
Complete the square: $x^{2}+2x+5 = (x+1)^{2}+4$. Let $w = x+1$:
\[
 \int \frac{dw}{(w^{2}+4)^{3/2}}.
\]
Let $w = 2\tan\theta$, $(w^{2}+4)^{3/2} = 8\sec^{3}\theta$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{2\sec^{2}\theta}{8\sec^{3}\theta}\,d\theta
 &= \frac{1}{4}\int \cos\theta\,d\theta
 = \frac{\sin\theta}{4} + C.
\end{aligned}$$
From the triangle: $\sin\theta = w/\sqrt{w^{2}+4} = (x+1)/\sqrt{x^{2}+2x+5}$.
\[
 \int \frac{dx}{(x^{2}+2x+5)^{3/2}} = \frac{x+1}{4\sqrt{x^{2}+2x+5}} + C.
\]

content.title: Trigonometric Substitution
content.content:
1. **Identify the form**: $\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}$,
 $\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}$, or $\sqrt{x^{2}-a^{2}}$.
 If none is present, try completing the square.

2. **Substitute**: $x = a\sin\theta$, $x = a\tan\theta$, or
 $x = a\sec\theta$ as appropriate.

3. **Simplify** the radical using the corresponding Pythagorean
 identity.

4. **Integrate** the resulting trigonometric integral (using
 techniques from Sections [sec:integration-by-parts]
 and [sec:trig-integrals] as needed).

5. **Convert back** to $x$ using a reference triangle.

content.text:
The hyperbolic functions $\sinh x$ and $\cosh x$ satisfy the identity
$\cosh^{2}x - \sinh^{2}x = 1$, which is analogous to the Pythagorean
identity. This makes them natural alternatives for Type 2 and Type 3
substitutions.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}$ via hyperbolic substitution
content.solution:
Let $x = a\sinh t$, so $dx = a\cosh t\,dt$ and
$\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}} = \sqrt{a^{2}\sinh^{2}t + a^{2}} = a\cosh t$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}
 &= \int \frac{a\cosh t}{a\cosh t}\,dt = \int dt = t + C \\
 &= \operatorname{arcsinh}\!\left(\frac{x}{a}\right) + C
 = \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right) + C_{1}.
\end{aligned}$$
The last step uses the identity
$\operatorname{arcsinh}(u) = \ln(u + \sqrt{u^{2}+1})$.

content.title: Euler and Trigonometric Substitution
content.content:
Leonhard Euler (1707–1783) systematized the use of trigonometric and
hyperbolic substitutions in his monumental *Introductio in analysin
infinitorum* (1748) and *Institutiones calculi integralis* (1768–1770).
His three-volume work on integral calculus catalogued techniques that
students still use today. Euler, one of the most prolific mathematicians in
history, was also a devout Christian who wrote on theology and apologetics.
His faith and his mathematics were not separate endeavors but expressions of
a single vocation.

content.title: The Geometry Behind the Substitutions
content.content:
Each trig substitution corresponds to a geometric parameterization. The
substitution $x = a\sin\theta$ parameterizes the upper semicircle of radius
$a$; the substitution $x = a\tan\theta$ parameterizes a hyperbola-like curve.
These connections between algebra and geometry were deeply important to
17th-century mathematicians like Descartes, who unified the two disciplines
in his *La G\'eom\'etrie* (1637).

marginNotes.text:
For as the heavens are higher than the earth,
so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts.

Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and
do not lean on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge him, and
he will make straight your paths.

The heavens declare the glory of God, and the
sky above proclaims his handiwork.

Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and
knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how inscrutable his
ways!

When I look at your heavens, the work of your
fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place, what is man
that you are mindful of him?

He has made everything beautiful in its
time. Also, he has put eternity into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find
out what God has done from the beginning to the end.

And God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding
beyond measure, and breadth of mind like the sand on the seashore.

marginNotes.author: Archimedes
marginNotes.text:
Give me a place to stand, and I shall move the
earth.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen of the sciences,
and number theory is the queen of mathematics.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Mathematicians are like Frenchmen: whatever you
say to them they translate into their own language and forthwith it is
something entirely different.
